one_line_takeaway: Wrapping an LLM robot controller in a data-driven reachability safety filter yields provable, zero-collision guarantees without a system model, at the cost of extra computation and an LLM-API-limited ~1.5 Hz control rate.
key_finding: Across all tested obstacle counts and planning horizons the reachability filter produced ZERO collisions (Table I), while running fast enough for real-time single-step verification (25 Hz down to 4.5 Hz as obstacles/horizon grow). Against the Advantage-based Intervention Safety Filter with an LLM controller (LLM-AISF) and the RL-SAILR baseline (Table II), the proposed filter is the only one offering a formal safety guarantee and the only one that guarantees collision avoidance; it also permits operating closer to obstacles (0.1 m minimum distance vs 0.5 m for LLM-AISF) because it verifies over a 3-step horizon instead of a single step, and needs no pre-training (unlike RL-SAILR). The stated trade-off is higher computational cost than LLM-AISF. The end-to-end update rate is capped at ~1.5 Hz by GPT-4o API rate limits (500 RPM / 30k TPM, ~230-token prompt), not by the safety layer.
core_contribution: A runtime safety-assurance framework that lets an LLM act as a high-level controller for a black-box robot while providing FORMAL collision-avoidance guarantees via data-driven reachability analysis. At each planning cycle the LLM (GPT-4o) proposes a short sequence of low-level control actions; a safety layer overapproximates the robot's forward reachable set from offline-collected noisy trajectory data (no analytical model), checks the reachable set against obstacles modeled as constrained zonotopes, and if a collision is possible uses projected gradient descent to adjust the plan or falls back to a failsafe braking maneuver (Algorithm 1). The paper explicitly builds on and adapts prior work: the data-driven reachability step (Algorithm 2) is adapted from Alanwar et al. [29], and the unsafe-action adjustment step (Algorithm 3) is from Selim et al. [14]; the black-box safe-RL reachability idea comes from [14],[18]. The paper's OWN novelty is extending this model-free reachability + safety-filter machinery to a probabilistic, zero-shot LLM controller and proving (Theorem 1, by induction) that the closed loop remains safe, rather than any new reachability algorithm.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the classical security sense; the primary hazard is the LLM controller itself. Because LLMs produce control commands through probabilistic reasoning with no formal guarantees, their outputs can misinterpret ambiguous instructions or violate safety constraints and drive the robot into obstacles. The paper motivates this with (cited, not its own) evidence that minor adversarial modifications to prompts or perceptual data can degrade performance by 19-29% [ref 1] and cites jailbreaking of LLM-controlled robots [ref 3]. The defense goal is to bound and correct ANY unsafe LLM-generated plan (whether from ambiguity, hallucination, or perturbation) so the robot provably never collides, assuming a safe start state, bounded process noise captured by a noise zonotope, static per-episode obstacles that are instantaneously sensed, and the ability to brake to a stop within n_brake steps.
limitations: Guarantees rest on strong assumptions: static obstacles that are instantaneously and fully sensed, bounded noise representable as a zonotope, a validly upper-bounded Lipschitz constant / lower-bounded covering radius from offline data holding at deployment, and a valid braking failsafe. Evaluation is small-scale case studies (one simulated TurtleBot3 in Gazebo, one real JetRacer) with no quantitative success-rate or task-completion metrics reported and no statistical trials. Real-time rate is throttled to ~1.5 Hz by the cloud LLM API. The authors note ChatGPT is highly sensitive to prompt wording (different prompts can cause planning failure), and they had to inflate the prompted velocity lower bound to [0.1, 0.5] m/s to stop GPT-4o outputting zero velocity. Stateless API use prevents feeding reachability feedback back to the LLM. Dynamic/multi-agent environments are explicitly out of scope.

datasets_used: 600-step offline noisy input/output trajectory data collected in an empty environment (no public benchmark dataset used)
TurtleBot3 simulated in Gazebo/ROS in two environments: 'world' (cubic obstacles) and 'house' (furnished indoor)
JetRacer real vehicle in a CPS lab with NOKOV motion capture (10x 9-megapixel cameras)
TurtleBot3 LiDAR: 19 range measurements over -45 to 45 deg (house), 37 measurements over -90 to 90 deg (world)
quantitative_results: Table I (no collision in any case): 3 obstacles / 3 plans -> 0.04 s, 25 Hz; 3 obs / 5 plans -> 0.1 s, 10 Hz; 3 obs / 10 plans -> 0.16 s, 6 Hz; 5 obs / 3 plans -> 0.08 s, 12.5 Hz; 5 obs / 5 plans -> 0.16 s, 6 Hz; 5 obs / 10 plans -> 0.22 s, 4.5 Hz

Table II: formal safety guarantee = Yes (ours) vs No (LLM-AISF) vs No (RL-SAILR); minimum distance to obstacle = 0.1 m (ours) vs 0.5 m (LLM-AISF); plan horizon = 3 steps (ours) vs 1 (LLM-AISF) vs 1 (RL-SAILR); pre-training = No/No/Yes; collision = No (ours) vs Yes (LLM-AISF) vs Yes (RL-SAILR)
GPT-4o at temperature 0.1; 3-step planning horizon (5 steps for JetRacer); prompt ~230 tokens
API limits 500 RPM (~8.3 Hz) and 30,000 TPM (~500 tokens/s) cap the achievable control loop at ~1.5 Hz
Control bounds: linear velocity [0.0, 0.1] m/s first step and [0.0, 0.5] m/s afterward; angular velocity [-0.5, 0.5] rad/s (prompt used inflated [0.1, 0.5] m/s to avoid zero output)
Compute for TurtleBot3: Intel i5 1235U CPU, 8 GB RAM
Cited (NOT the paper's own result): adversarial prompt/perception perturbations degrade performance by 19-29% [ref 1]
baselines_compared: LLM-AISF: Advantage-based Intervention Safety Filter with an LLM as controller, within the SAILR framework [31]
RL-SAILR: Safe Advantage-based Intervention for Learning Policies with Reinforcement [31]
